label: delphi-r1-p2
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Vista Camino (invented; demographics roughly 78% non-Hispanic white against a metro region at 52%) owns a 4.2-acre former corporation yard appraised at $11.8M. The council approved, 4–1, an exclusive negotiating agreement and then a disposition and development agreement conveying the parcel to Pacifica Communities LLC, a private developer, for $1, in exchange for: 120 housing units of which 30% (36 units) are deed-restricted affordable — 18 at low-income and 18 at moderate-income levels — with 25-year covenants; a half-acre publicly accessible plaza built and maintained by the developer; and a "community preference" covenant giving 50% of the affordable units priority to applicants who have lived or worked in Vista Camino for at least five years.

The city declared the parcel "exempt surplus land" and did not send notices of availability to housing sponsors before entering exclusive negotiations. The Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) has issued a notice of violation asserting the disposition does not qualify for the claimed exemption (citing the affordability mix and the 25-year covenant term) and warning of penalties of 30% of the disposition price or fair market value. A taxpayer association has sued alleging an unconstitutional gift of public funds (Cal. Const. art. XVI, § 6) given the $11.8M-to-$1 spread, and a regional fair-housing organization has threatened suit alleging the five-year local preference has an unlawful disparate impact given the city's demographics.

## The question

Does the disposition violate the Surplus Land Act (Gov. Code §§ 54220–54234) — was the parcel eligible for any 'exempt surplus land' category given the 30% low/moderate mix and 25-year covenants, what process was owed, and what do HCD's enforcement position and the 30% penalty mean for the deal? Is $1 plus in-kind public benefits adequate consideration under the gift clause, or is the public-purpose doctrine enough? Does the local-preference covenant violate the FHA/FEHA under a disparate-impact theory or AFFH obligations, and can it be saved by redesign? How do the three exposures interact, and how should the deal be restructured?

## Proposition to assess

"If challenged as structured, the disposition would be held unlawful under the Surplus Land Act (i.e., HCD's position that the parcel did not qualify as exempt surplus land and that the required process was violated would substantially prevail)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the leading cases and how courts have actually applied the rule to messy facts.
text: {"probability":82,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"On these facts, HCD's position that the parcel was not \"exempt surplus land\" and that the notice/negotiation process was owed and violated is very likely correct and would substantially prevail if litigated, because the deal has two independent, objectively measurable defects that go to the heart of the only exemption category that could plausibly apply (the affordable-housing exemption), and no other Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) exemption fits a for-$1 conveyance of a decommissioned corporation yard to a private for-profit developer.","rationales":["The 25-year affordability covenant is well short of the ~55-year (rental)/45-year (ownership) minimum duration that the affordable-housing exempt-surplus-land category in Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) requires, and covenant duration is an objective fact not subject to much interpretive give.","Only 18 of 120 units (15%) are restricted to 'lower income' households, which falls below the 30%-lower-income threshold the exemption demands, since moderate-income units (80-120% AMI) generally do not count toward the lower-income percentage under the income definitions the Act incorporates.","No other § 54221(f)(1) exemption category (public-to-public transfer, pre-2020 grandfathered negotiations, physically infeasible-for-housing site, small-parcel exemption) plausibly covers a $1 conveyance of a 4.2-acre former corporation yard to a private LLC, leaving the city with no fallback exemption theory.","Because the affordable-housing exemption fails on its face, the predicate for skipping Gov. Code § 54222 notices-of-availability and § 54223 priority negotiation with housing sponsors was never satisfied, so the procedural violation is essentially derivative of and reinforces the substantive exemption failure rather than being a separate close question.","HCD's post-AB 1486 enforcement posture has generally been assertive and has driven cities into settlement/compliance rather than cities winning exemption arguments outright, and the hypothetical's framing (HCD citing exactly the mix and covenant-term defects) mirrors the agency's real enforcement pattern, suggesting the legal and practical environment leans toward vindicating HCD's reading."],"authorities":["Gov. Code §§ 54220-54234 (Surplus Land Act, as substantially amended by AB 1486, Stats. 2019, ch. 664)","Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) (definitions of 'exempt surplus land,' including the affordable-housing category with its minimum affordability-percentage and covenant-duration requirements — verify current exact subsection letter and thresholds given post-2019 amendments such as AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135, AB 480)","Gov. Code § 54222 (notice of availability to housing sponsors and other entities before disposition)","Gov. Code § 54223 (priority negotiation period for entities proposing affordable housing)","Gov. Code § 54230.5 (penalties for SLA violations — 30%/50% of greater of disposition price or fair market value, and voidability of noncompliant dispositions — verify current percentage tiers and voidability language)","Health & Safety Code §§ 50079.5, 50093, 50105 (statutory definitions of 'lower income,' 'moderate income,' and related affordability terms incorporated by reference into the SLA exemption analysis)","HCD Surplus Land Act technical assistance/guidance materials and published enforcement letters (e.g., against other California cities) as persuasive, non-binding indicators of HCD's interpretive approach — verify specific examples before citing"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether I am correctly recalling the precise, currently operative statutory text and subsection of Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) — specifically the exact covenant-duration minimum (approx. 55 years rental/45 years ownership) and the exact lower-income percentage threshold (and whether moderate-income units can ever be blended in) — given that the Surplus Land Act has been amended multiple times since AB 1486 in 2019, and whether some other, less-obvious exemption category might arguably apply to these facts that I have not identified.\""}

data.position_summary: On these facts, HCD's position that the parcel was not "exempt surplus land" and that the notice/negotiation process was owed and violated is very likely correct and would substantially prevail if litigated, because the deal has two independent, objectively measurable defects that go to the heart of the only exemption category that could plausibly apply (the affordable-housing exemption), and no other Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) exemption fits a for-$1 conveyance of a decommissioned corporation yard to a private for-profit developer.
rationales: The 25-year affordability covenant is well short of the ~55-year (rental)/45-year (ownership) minimum duration that the affordable-housing exempt-surplus-land category in Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) requires, and covenant duration is an objective fact not subject to much interpretive give.

Only 18 of 120 units (15%) are restricted to 'lower income' households, which falls below the 30%-lower-income threshold the exemption demands, since moderate-income units (80-120% AMI) generally do not count toward the lower-income percentage under the income definitions the Act incorporates.

No other § 54221(f)(1) exemption category (public-to-public transfer, pre-2020 grandfathered negotiations, physically infeasible-for-housing site, small-parcel exemption) plausibly covers a $1 conveyance of a 4.2-acre former corporation yard to a private LLC, leaving the city with no fallback exemption theory.

Because the affordable-housing exemption fails on its face, the predicate for skipping Gov. Code § 54222 notices-of-availability and § 54223 priority negotiation with housing sponsors was never satisfied, so the procedural violation is essentially derivative of and reinforces the substantive exemption failure rather than being a separate close question.

HCD's post-AB 1486 enforcement posture has generally been assertive and has driven cities into settlement/compliance rather than cities winning exemption arguments outright, and the hypothetical's framing (HCD citing exactly the mix and covenant-term defects) mirrors the agency's real enforcement pattern, suggesting the legal and practical environment leans toward vindicating HCD's reading.
authorities: Gov. Code §§ 54220-54234 (Surplus Land Act, as substantially amended by AB 1486, Stats. 2019, ch. 664)

Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) (definitions of 'exempt surplus land,' including the affordable-housing category with its minimum affordability-percentage and covenant-duration requirements — verify current exact subsection letter and thresholds given post-2019 amendments such as AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135, AB 480)
Gov. Code § 54222 (notice of availability to housing sponsors and other entities before disposition)
Gov. Code § 54223 (priority negotiation period for entities proposing affordable housing)

Gov. Code § 54230.5 (penalties for SLA violations — 30%/50% of greater of disposition price or fair market value, and voidability of noncompliant dispositions — verify current percentage tiers and voidability language)
Health & Safety Code §§ 50079.5, 50093, 50105 (statutory definitions of 'lower income,' 'moderate income,' and related affordability terms incorporated by reference into the SLA exemption analysis)

HCD Surplus Land Act technical assistance/guidance materials and published enforcement letters (e.g., against other California cities) as persuasive, non-binding indicators of HCD's interpretive approach — verify specific examples before citing
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether I am correctly recalling the precise, currently operative statutory text and subsection of Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1) — specifically the exact covenant-duration minimum (approx. 55 years rental/45 years ownership) and the exact lower-income percentage threshold (and whether moderate-income units can ever be blended in) — given that the Surplus Land Act has been amended multiple times since AB 1486 in 2019, and whether some other, less-obvious exemption category might arguably apply to these facts that I have not identified."
